steps.why: Past perfect — the flashback, before another past event. Camera 2!

steps.text: is / are + -ing
steps.why: Present progressive — live action, right now! Camera 3!

steps.text: was / were + -ing
steps.why: Past progressive — ongoing past action that got interrupted. Camera 4! ✓

subConcepts.name: Standard English — Getting It Right in Writing

learningGoal: How to spot things we say every day that are actually wrong in writing
How to fix the most common writing mistakes so your English sounds polished

screens.title: Daisy's writing check
screens.body:
Daisy is checking her story for mistakes her teacher always marks. Can you spot what's wrong here?

**"I done my homework before tea."**

visualProps.sentence: I done my homework before tea.

screens.title: What we say vs what we write
screens.body: Lots of things we say every day are actually **wrong** in writing. Here are the fixes:

steps.text: Wrong: "I done my homework before tea."
steps.why: This sounds OK when speaking, but it's wrong in writing

steps.text: Right: "I did my homework before tea."
steps.why: 'Done' always needs a helper word before it (like 'has done' or 'had done'). On its own, say **'did'**.

steps.text: Wrong: "We was going to the park."
steps.why: Another very common mistake

steps.text: Right: "We were going to the park."
steps.why: 'Was' is for one person (I was, he was). 'Were' is for more than one (we were, they were).

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Choose the correct word: 'She ___ her best in the race.'

options: done
did
does
doing
has do
interaction.correctAnswer: did
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'She did' is correct. Remember: 'done' needs a helper word — you can say 'she has done' but never 'she done' on its own. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'She did' is correct. Remember: 'done' needs a helper word — you can say 'she has done' but never 'she done' on its own.

screens.title: Standard English — sorted!
screens.body: Remember these rules when you're writing:

steps.text: 1. 'Did' not 'done' on its own
steps.why: 'I did it' ✓ — 'I done it' ✗

steps.text: 2. 'Were' not 'was' with we/they
steps.why: 'We were' ✓ — 'We was' ✗

steps.text: 3. 'Have' not 'of' after should/could/would
steps.why: 'Should have' ✓ — 'Should of' ✗

steps.text: 4. 'I' when doing, 'me' when it's done to you
steps.why: Remove the other person to check which sounds right

learningGoal: How to spot common spoken English mistakes hiding in written sentences
Why some things that sound perfectly normal are actually wrong in writing

screens.title: Can you spot the mistakes?
screens.body:
Ella is proofreading her friend's story. This sentence has several things wrong — how many can you find?

**"Me and Amira gone to the shops and we buyed some sweets."**

visualProps.sentence: Me and Amira gone to the shops and we buyed some sweets.

screens.title: Here are the mistakes
screens.body: Let's fix each one:

steps.text: Mistake 1: 'Me and Amira' should be 'Amira and I'
Mistake 2: 'gone' should be 'went'
Mistake 3: 'buyed' should be 'bought'
Corrected: "Amira and I went to the shops and we bought some sweets."
steps.why: Now it's proper written English!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which sentence is correct?

options: Me and Tom done our homework.
Tom and me did our homework.
Tom and I did our homework.
Me and Tom did our homework.
Tom and I done our homework.
interaction.correctAnswer: Tom and I did our homework.
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Tom and I' (not 'me and Tom') because 'I' is doing the action. 'Did' (not 'done') because 'done' needs a helper word. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Tom and I' (not 'me and Tom') because 'I' is doing the action. 'Did' (not 'done') because 'done' needs a helper word.

steps.text: 1. Me and Tom done our homework.
2. Tom and me did our homework.
3. Tom and I did our homework.
4. Me and Tom did our homework.
5. Tom and I done our homework.

screens.title: You're a proofreading pro!
screens.body: When checking writing, watch out for these sneaky mistakes:

steps.text: 'Me and...' at the start
steps.why: Change to '... and I' (remove the other person to check)

steps.text: 'Done', 'seen', 'gone' without a helper word
steps.why: Should be 'did', 'saw', 'went' on their own

steps.text: 'Should of', 'could of', 'would of'
steps.why: Always 'should have', 'could have', 'would have'

steps.text: 'Don't' with he/she/it
steps.why: Should be 'doesn't'

learningGoal: How to choose the right verb when sneaky words try to trick you
Why 'neither...nor' and 'a number of' follow special rules

screens.title: Evie's grammar puzzle
screens.body:
Evie keeps getting tripped up by sentences with extra words in the middle.

Which is correct?

**"The basket of oranges are on the table."** or **"The basket of oranges is on the table."**

visualProps.sentence: The basket of oranges are on the table.

screens.title: The trick to getting it right
screens.body: Ignore the 'of...' part — find the real subject

steps.text: Wrong: "The basket of oranges are on the table."
steps.why: This is wrong because...

steps.text: Right: "The basket of oranges is on the table."
steps.why: Cross out 'of oranges' in your head. What's left? 'The basket ___ on the table.' Basket is one thing, so it's **'is'**.

steps.text: The rule: Ignore the 'of...' part — find the real subject
steps.why: Remember this for the exam!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Choose the correct word: 'The packet of crisps ___ already been opened.'

options: have
are
has
were
is
interaction.correctAnswer: has
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The subject is 'packet' (one thing), not 'crisps'. Cross out 'of crisps' — 'the packet has been opened'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The subject is 'packet' (one thing), not 'crisps'. Cross out 'of crisps' — 'the packet has been opened'.

visualProps.sentence: 'The packet of crisps ___ already been opened.'

screens.title: Tricky agreement — mastered!
screens.body: Three sneaky patterns to watch for:

steps.text: 1. 'Of...' phrases: cross them out!
steps.why: 'The basket of oranges IS...' — basket is the real subject

steps.text: 2. Neither/nor: match the NEAREST word
steps.why: 'Neither the captain nor the players WERE...'

steps.text: 3. Group words: one unit = 'is', acting separately = 'are'
steps.why: 'The team IS winning' but 'The jury WERE unable to agree'

learningGoal: How to spot when a sentence has the wrong verb because of a tricky subject

screens.title: Can you spot the mistake?
screens.body:
Daisy is proofreading sentences. Something is wrong here:

**"One of my friends are moving to a new school next term."**

visualProps.sentence: One of my friends are moving to a new school next term.

screens.title: Here's the mistake
screens.body: 'Are' should be 'is' — the subject is 'one' (one friend), not 'friends'.

steps.text: Wrong: "One of my friends are moving to a new school next term."
steps.why: 'Are' should be 'is' — the subject is 'one' (one friend), not 'friends'.

steps.text: Right: "One of my friends is moving to a new school next term."
steps.why: Now the verb matches the real subject!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which sentence is correct?

options: The box of chocolates were lovely.
The box of chocolates was lovely.
The box of chocolates are lovely.
The box of chocolates have been lovely.
The box of chocolates be lovely.
interaction.correctAnswer: The box of chocolates was lovely.
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The subject is 'box' (one thing). Cross out 'of chocolates' and it's clear: 'the box was lovely'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The subject is 'box' (one thing). Cross out 'of chocolates' and it's clear: 'the box was lovely'.

steps.text: 1. The box of chocolates were lovely.
2. The box of chocolates was lovely.
3. The box of chocolates are lovely.
4. The box of chocolates have been lovely.
5. The box of chocolates be lovely.

screens.title: You're a mistake-spotting expert!
screens.body: Always check: what is the REAL subject of the sentence?

steps.text: Cross out 'of...' phrases
steps.why: They hide the real subject

steps.text: With neither/nor: look at the nearest word
steps.why: The verb matches whichever word is closest

steps.text: THE number of = one thing. A number of = many
steps.why: This is a favourite exam trick!

subConcepts.name: Future Perfect — Finished Before a Deadline

learningGoal: How to talk about things that will be **finished** before a future time
How to spot the trigger words that tell you this special verb form is needed

screens.title: Daisy's future deadline
screens.body:
Daisy is talking about her 11+ preparation.

**"By next September, she will have completed all her preparation."**

This sentence talks about something that will be **finished before** a future time.

visualProps.sentence: By next September, she will have completed all her preparation.

screens.title: How to build this verb form
screens.body: It's actually really simple — just three words put together:

steps.text: The pattern: will + have + done-word
steps.why: Three parts put together

steps.text: The trigger: 'By next September'
steps.why: When you see 'by + a future time', that's your clue!

steps.text: Wrong: "By next September, she will complete all her preparation."
steps.why: 'Will complete' just says she'll do it sometime. 'Will have completed' means she'll have **already finished** by that date.

steps.text: Right: "By next September, she will have completed all her preparation."
steps.why: This makes it clear it'll be DONE by the deadline

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Choose the correct form: 'By the end of this term, we ___ all our times tables.'

options: learn
learned
will learn
will have learned
are learning
interaction.correctAnswer: will have learned
interaction.correctFeedback: Amazing! 'Will have learned' tells us the learning will be **finished** before the end of term. The 'by' is the clue! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Will have learned' tells us the learning will be **finished** before the end of term. The 'by' is the clue!

visualProps.sentence: 'By the end of this term, we ___ all our times tables.'

screens.title: Future perfect — nailed it!
screens.body: Here's your quick recipe:

steps.text: Pattern: will + have + done-word
steps.why: e.g. 'will have finished', 'will have completed'

steps.text: Trigger: 'by + future time'
steps.why: 'By Friday', 'By next year', 'By the time...'

steps.text: It means: completely DONE before the deadline
steps.why: Not just happening — finished!

learningGoal: How to spot when a sentence needs 'will have' instead of just 'will'

screens.title: Can you spot the mistake?
screens.body:
Jake is checking his writing. Something isn't quite right:

**"By the end of the week, I will finish my project."**

visualProps.sentence: By the end of the week, I will finish my project.

screens.title: Here's the mistake
screens.body: 'Will finish' should be 'will have finished' because 'by the end of the week' tells us there's a deadline.

steps.text: Wrong: "By the end of the week, I will finish my project."
steps.why: 'Will finish' should be 'will have finished' because 'by the end of the week' tells us there's a deadline.

steps.text: Right: "By the end of the week, I will have finished my project."
steps.why: Now it's clear the action is finished before the deadline

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which sentence is correct?

options: By June, she will pass all her exams.
By June, she will have passed all her exams.
By June, she will had passed all her exams.
By June, she will has passed all her exams.
By June, she will been passed all her exams.
interaction.correctAnswer: By June, she will have passed all her exams.
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! 'By June' is the trigger — it tells us the exams need to be DONE by then, so it's 'will have passed'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly there! 'By June' is the trigger — it tells us the exams need to be DONE by then, so it's 'will have passed'.

steps.text: 1. By June, she will pass all her exams.
2. By June, she will have passed all her exams.
3. By June, she will had passed all her exams.
4. By June, she will has passed all her exams.
5. By June, she will been passed all her exams.

screens.title: Deadline detector — activated!
screens.body: Whenever you see 'by + future time', check if you need 'will have':

steps.text: Spot the trigger: 'by Friday', 'by next year', 'by the time...'
steps.why: These all mean there's a deadline

steps.text: Use: will + have + done-word
steps.why: 'Will have finished', 'will have completed'

steps.text: It means: completely DONE before the deadline
steps.why: That's the future perfect!
